items.q: In programming, does x = 5 ask whether x equals 5?
items.correct: No - in most languages a single = is assignment, storing 5 in x; comparison uses a different operator such as ==.
items.misconception: Yes - the equals sign always tests equality like in maths.
items.random_wrong: Yes - x = 5 deletes the variable x.
items.why: Maths notation collides with code. Reading assignment aloud as 'x becomes 5' breaks the habit and explains why x = x + 1 is legal in code but absurd in algebra.

items.q: Can a variable hold several values at the same time?
items.correct: No - a simple variable holds exactly one value; assigning a new value overwrites the old one. Storing many values needs a structure such as a list or array.
items.misconception: Yes - a variable remembers every value it has ever been given.
items.random_wrong: Yes - variables store one value per letter of their name.
items.why: Trace tables expose this: each assignment replaces the content. The 'history' students imagine is exactly what the trace table exists to record externally.

items.q: Does a loop with the condition WHILE x < 10 always run exactly 10 times?
items.correct: No - how many times it runs depends on x's starting value and how x changes inside the loop; it may run zero times or forever.
items.misconception: Yes - the number in the condition is the number of repetitions.
items.random_wrong: Yes - while loops always run at least 10 times.
items.why: Students pattern-match the 10 to a repeat count. Only tracing the variable shows the true behaviour - including the infinite loop when x never reaches 10.

items.q: A variable is best described as:
items.correct: A named memory location whose stored value can change as the program runs
items.misconception: A fixed number that can never change
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A named memory location whose stored value can change as the program runs. “A fixed number that can never change” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which construct executes different statements depending on a condition?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Selection (if/else). “Sequence” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A for loop is most appropriate when:
items.correct: The number of repetitions is known in advance
items.misconception: The program should never repeat anything
items.random_wrong: You want the code to run only if a condition is false
items.why: Correct answer: The number of repetitions is known in advance. “The program should never repeat anything” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What distinguishes a function from a procedure?
items.correct: A function returns a value to the code that called it
items.random_wrong: A procedure runs faster than any function
items.why: It's A function returns a value to the code that called it. “A function cannot take parameters” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: total starts at 0. A loop runs FOR i = 1 TO 5 and adds i to total each time. What is total at the end?
items.why: Exact answer: 15. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
